content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

There’s a second, quieter kind of AI product that doesn’t chat with you — it reads a pile of your documents and turns them into something useful. Building one taught me that the model is maybe twenty percent of the work. The other eighty percent is everything around the model: getting the right information in and trusting what comes out. Here’s the pipeline:
Step #1: Be picky about what you let in.
The instinct is to feed the model everything. That’s a mistake. Garbage in the pile means garbage in the results, plus wasted cost processing it. So, before anything else, a cheap, fast model acts as a bouncer: for each document, does this actually belong here? Only the relevant material goes through. And to avoid paying for that judgment over and over, I remember the verdict — tied to the document’s exact contents — so re-running the process skips everything already decided. (When I improve the bouncer’s instructions, I bump a version marker, which automatically forces a fresh look at everything. Cheap to skip, easy to redo on purpose.)
Step #2: Break big documents into bite-sized pieces.
Models can’t usefully swallow a fifty-page file whole. So each document gets cut into overlapping chunks small enough to handle, with a little…
